headlines.title: Newsom signs bill making May 17 official Bruce Lee Day in California
headlines.description: Gov. Gavin Newsom signed a bill Tuesday officially recognizing May 17 as Bruce Lee Day in California, honoring the San Francisco-born actor and cultural figure. The commemorative day will make Bruce Lee the first Chinese American to be honored with a day in California, according to Assemblymember Matt Haney, who introduced the bill in February “Bruce Lee represents the very best of California: innovation, diversity, determination, and the courage to challenge convention,” Haney said in a press release announcing the signing of the bill. “Born in San Francisco, he transformed martial arts, redefined Hollywood, and inspired millions around the world to pursue their potential. At a time when Asian Americans were too often absent from or stereotyped on screen, Bruce Lee helped generations see themselves represented with strength and dignity.” Lee was born in San Francisco in 1940 and raised in Hong Kong. He became a child actor in Chinese films and later became an international star most known for martial arts. Haney’s press release said he returned to San Francisco at the age of 18 on May 17, 1959, to expand his career and global impact. Lee became a cultural icon and filmmaker who became known for his philosophy of adaptability, discipline and self-expression, the release said. “To be the first Chinese American recognized this way is a testament to my father’s enduring legacy and the countless lives he continues to touch.” Shannon Lee, founder of the Bruce Lee Foundation and Bruce Lee’s daughter, said in the release. “From young people who found confidence and possibility in his philosophy, to families who finally saw themselves represented on screen, to athletes who still draw on his teachings of discipline and inner strength, his reach is profound. My father was a bridge between cultures through his courage, and his spirit of interracial solidarity remains as relevant as ever.” The law’s text said the commemorative day will encourage school lessons, cultural exhibits and public events to highlight Lee’s impact and accomplishments. The Chinese Historical Society of America is currently displaying its “We are Bruce Lee: Under the Sky, One Family” exhibit at its museum in San Francisco’s Chinatown. Haney said he hopes the annual day of commemoration will inspire people from any background to “have the power to change the world.” The first Bruce Lee Day in California will be celebrated on May 17, 2027.

headlines.title: Bay Area Host Committee announces plans to become permanent sports leadership commission
headlines.description: The Bay Area Host Committee, which helped coordinate the World Cup and Super Bowl in the region, has announced plans to make the committee a permanent regional leadership commission that will continue to bring sports events to the Bay Area for years to come. The initiative announced Tuesday will be led by its inaugural Leadership Council, which will bring together some of the Bay Area’s biggest executives, politicians and businessmen to create a cohort that will continue the committee’s work to bring major sports events to the region. Speaking on the committee’s future, BAHC officials voiced excitement to bid for more sports events in the future — like the 2031 FIFA Women’s World Cup, which will be hosted by the United States, Mexico, Costa Rica and Jamaica — with the hope of building upon the momentum created over the last two calendar years. “We’ve proven the Bay Area can execute as a unified region on the world’s biggest stage,” Larry Baer, who serves on the BAHC’s Board of Directors as president and CEO of the San Francisco Giants, said in a statement. “The Leadership Council ensures we preserve the relationships, expertise and momentum we’ve built, and it positions us to write the next chapter of Bay Area sports history.” Following the committee’s announcement of its permanent leadership cohort, the BAHC joined San Francisco and U.S. soccer leaders Tuesday night to watch the Mexico-Ecuador match in downtown San Francisco. The watch party celebrated the work the BAHC has done to bring major sporting events to the region, coming one night before the Bay Area will host its final World Cup match, a round of 32 bout between the United States and Bosnia-Herzegovina in Santa Clara. Gathered together, regional leaders and event organizers remarked upon what has been a landmark summer for the Bay Area, highlighted by increases in tourism and economic activity. In May, SF Travel, the official marketing organization of San Francisco, projected the city will welcome 24.2 million visitors in 2026, exceeding its 2025 mark of 23.7 million. SF Travel also projected visitor spending will reach $9.9 billion, which would exceed the city’s pre-pandemic record of $9.6 billion in 2019. The BAHC also projects that the NBA All-Star Weekend held in San Francisco in 2025, combined with the Super Bowl in February and the World Cup matches for the past month, will bring $1.6 billion in economic activity for the region. San Francisco Mayor Daniel Lurie, who previously led the San Francisco Bay Area Super Bowl 50 Host Committee when Levi’s Stadium hosted the big football game in 2016, was especially complimentary of the impact the BAHC’s events have had on his city. “The eyes of the world are on us,” said Lurie, speaking on how the World Cup showcases San Francisco and the Bay Area to a global audience. “Visitors from all over the world come here for a few days, and then they go back, they report to their friends and family and they’re like, ‘Oh, we gotta go visit San Francisco in the Bay Area again.'” The mayor’s office has worked to capitalize on this tourism to fuel the city’s revitalization, outlining safety, transportation and cleanliness as city priorities ahead of the World Cup. Regional leaders and organizers also celebrated the collaboration required to bring major sporting events to the Bay Area, highlighting partnerships among local governments, business leaders and sports organizations “I need local government,” said Zaileen Janmohamed, president and CEO of the BAHC, in an interview alongside Lurie. “I need all the sports teams to come together to really pull this thing off, because it literally has not been done before and it doesn’t work unless everyone comes together to make it happen.” Moving forward, the BAHC will aim to capitalize upon these established connections, with the intent to help the Bay Area host one major sports event every two or three years moving forward.

headlines.title: Did the milk expire? California bans ‘sell by' food labels to cut food waste
headlines.description: In Kimberley Kausen’s home, a passed “sell by” date on a jug of milk means different things to different family members. For her daughter, it means the jug belongs in the trash. For her husband, it means the milk is still good for a few more days. Kausen, a chef and cooking teacher in Irvine, California, is more discerning and often uses her sense of smell before deciding what to do with the milk. “I’ll put some thought into it, and if we’re talking about meat and poultry, I’m very cautious about that and for sure will do the smell test and the touch test,” she said. The debate playing out in Kausen’s kitchen is repeated in homes across California and the country, where varying phrases on food packaging have long left shoppers unsure whether food is simply past its peak quality or unsafe to eat. The state is aiming to cut down on confusion — and the food waste it creates when people throw away food early — with a new food labeling law starting Wednesday. It bans the use of “sell by” labels on food packaging, which experts say act as a guide for retailers on how long to display products on the shelves but are not an indicator of whether they are still safe to consume. Now, manufacturers selling food in California must use two standardized labels — a “Best if Used By” label for peak quality and “Use By” label for product safety. Food manufacturers can choose to use either label or both, said Democratic Assemblymember Jacqui Irwin, the author of the bill. California became the first state in the U.S. to standardize food labels when it approved the law in 2024 that seeks to reduce food waste and the state’s climate-warming emissions. New York state lawmakers recently approved a similar law that is awaiting Gov. Kathy Hochul’s signature. Legislation addressing food labeling also has been proposed in Illinois, Maryland, Massachusetts, New Jersey and South Carolina, though it has not passed in those states. Nick Lapis, director of advocacy at Californians Against Waste, which co-sponsored the bill, said food labels are the leading cause of household food waste. The “sell by” date labels have also been a problem for food banks in California because people consider those dates as meaning the food has expired, he said. “We don’t need to build some kind of huge infrastructure and invest tons of money to solve this. We just need companies to use the same words across brands,” he said. There are more than 50 different date labels on packaged food sold in stores, according to a 2022 report on food waste published by the University of Maryland. The information in the labels is largely unregulated and often does not relate to food safety. “Consumers get confused and they just default to assuming that whatever date is on the package means ‘don’t eat it and throw it away’,” said Kumar Chandran, policy director at ReFED, a nonprofit focused on reducing food waste. Chandran said California and New York’s approval of food-labeling laws has added momentum to the push for a national standard. A bipartisan bill that would establish uniform food labels is pending in Congress. The U.S. Department of Agriculture recommended a decade ago that food sellers should switch to “Best if Used By” labeling. Currently, the only product that is regulated federally with date labels is infant formula. With no federal regulations dictating what information labels should include, the stamps have led to consumer confusion — and nearly 20% of the nation’s food waste, according to the Food and Drug Administration. In California, that’s about 6 million tons of unexpired food that’s tossed in the trash each year. Nate Rose, a spokesperson for the California Grocers Association, said some grocers have had to overhaul their labeling systems, but as a whole, the association has been supportive of the change. The new labels will result in “a win-win where we can reduce food waste and consumers will find these decisions a little bit simpler,” he said, adding that shoppers will still find old labels in stores for months to come as grocers sell through the products that already have them.
headlines.source: Olga R. Rodriguez | The Associated Press

headlines.title: 2 shot, wounded after San Francisco World Cup watch party
headlines.description: Two people were shot after a fight near a World Cup watch party in San Francisco’s Mission Bay neighborhood Tuesday night. The shooting happened around 9 p.m. near Mission Bay Boulevard and Fourth Street. Video from the scene showed crime tape surrounding Spark Social SF, which had hosted a World Cup watch party earlier in the evening. The San Francisco Chronicle reported an argument broke out during the final minutes of a DJ set before a man pulled out a gun and opened fire. Officers found two people suffering from gunshot wounds. According to the Chronicle, both victims were taken to a hospital and are expected to survive. The suspected shooter fled the scene. No arrests had been made as of Wednesday morning.

headlines.title: 2 stabbed, multiple arrested after Mexico World Cup watch party in San Jose
headlines.description: Two people were stabbed and multiple others were arrested after a World Cup watch party in downtown San Jose where thousands gathered to watch Mexico defeat Ecuador 2-0 and advance to the Round of 16 of the 2026 FIFA World Cup, police said. Excitement had overflowed in San Pedro Square as Mexico achieved a historic victory at Mexico City Stadium. “I feel very proud… my country… the people… I have no words,” one fan said. Another fan said, “Feels amazing. We’re the underdogs right now… we’re making history.” Organizers said about 30,000 fans packed San Pedro Square, where four giant screens were set up for the World Cup matches. The celebration became rowdy after the game ended. Just before midnight, San Jose police ordered people to disperse outside San Pedro Square. Video shot near Santa Clara Street and Market Street showed officers in riot gear lining the streets. Police said multiple people were arrested and booked into the Santa Clara County Main Jail on various charges. In a statement, San Jose police said the overwhelming majority of attendees celebrated responsibly, but numerous people engaged in disorderly and dangerous behavior as the night went on. Officers responded to multiple fights, fireworks being set off in crowded areas and other incidents. Police said two separate altercations resulted in stabbings. The victims suffered injuries that were not considered life-threatening. Police also said officers responded after a crowd surrounded an ambulance and several people climbed onto the vehicle, interfering with emergency responders. Authorities said members of the crowd threw bottles at officers as they worked to restore order.

headlines.title: Baby, mother found in Los Angeles after Hayward Amber Alert
headlines.description: A mother and baby were found in Los Angeles after an Amber Alert was issued for the 3-month-old boy out of Hayward, the California Highway Patrol said. California authorities issued early Wednesday an Amber Alert for the infant after it was believed to have been abducted. The California Highway Patrol issued the alert on behalf of the Hayward Police Department for Alameda, San Joaquin, Kern and Los Angeles counties. No other information was immediately available. This story uses functionality that may not work in our app. Click here to open the story in your web browser.

headlines.title: Transit price changes take effect across Bay Area
headlines.description: A number of transit fees and tolls are ticking up Wednesday across the Bay Area. The changes will cost travelers anywhere from 10 cents to nearly $2, depending on where they are traveling in the Bay Area. Drivers crossing the Golden Gate Bridge will pay more as a new round of toll increases takes effect. Golden Gate Bridge toll rates are increasing by 50 cents as part of the third year of a planned five-year annual toll hike. A toll invoice will now cost $11.25, while drivers with FasTrak or Pay As You Go accounts will pay lower rates, depending on their payment method. The Golden Gate Bridge, Highway and Transportation District said that without additional local or state funding, it must rely on toll revenue to help pay for bridge maintenance. NBC Bay Area’s Ginger Conejero Saab has the full report in the video above.

headlines.title: Fan calls ‘bulls–‘ on StubHub ‘upgrade' that moved World Cup seat 23 rows back
headlines.description: Several viewers tell NBC Bay Area Responds they’re having to play serious offense just to keep their pricey seats for Wednesday’s United States-Bosnia and Herzegovina World Cup match in Santa Clara. Sanjay Kalra in Saratoga, Matthew Gaubatz in San Francisco and Cristina Molina in the South Bay tell NBC Bay Area Responds they are not happy with ticket re-seller StubHub. “I was so mad,” Kalra said. “Very frustrating,” said Gaubatz. “I’m sorry, this is bulls—,” Molina said. Molina and her husband Jeremy, who own a mom-and-pop company called Elite Drywall in Mountain View, paid a pretty penny on StubHub for a prime perch: premium upper-deck tickets to see Team USA play in Santa Clara. “First row,” she said. “And it was like $2,200 for both…Why not? When’s the next World Cup gonna be here?” Then came a surprise note from StubHub. “They’re like, ‘Hey, we upgraded you guys,’” she said. Was it really an upgrade? Molina and her husband were sitting in row one of section 419. StubHub moved them to section 411, closer to center field. That’s likely a more expensive section. But Molina was no longer in row one, where she wanted to sit. Her supposedly “upgraded” seats were now up in row 24. “I was like, ‘How is that an upgrade?’ Now we’re two rows away from the last row,” she said. Kalra, in Saratoga, said he paid $2,300 on StubHub for five tickets back in November. Suddenly, this past week, the seller backed out. “I was really shocked,” Kalra said. “I feel they are fleecing the customers.” Gaubatz, in San Francisco, spent $2,800 on StubHub in April for three seats together. When his tickets arrived, only two seats were together. “One was completely on the other side of the stadium,” he said. “We just felt so powerless.” World Cup Jun 18 World Cup Jun 23 Kalra, Molina and Gaubatz are not alone. News reports around the country feature fans in similar situations. So, what’s going on? StubHub told NBC Bay Area Responds users’ issues are “largely transfer problems, not ticket problems.” StubHub accused FIFA’s ticket system of “significant performance issues that have affected transfers across all resale platforms.” FIFA said it, “rejects any suggestion that the functional issues being experienced by…third-party platforms…are the result of FIFA’s ticketing infrastructure.” NBC Bay Area Responds turned to Brian Hess with the Sports Fan Coalition, a national nonprofit consumer advocacy group. “We’re not hearing a lot of positive things about the ticketing experience for fans of the World Cup,” he said. Hess encourages World Cup ticket buyers to flag issues early. And stand your ground for replacement seats instead of refunds. “Demand they get you in the door. Fight for that,” Hess said. The fans, Molina, Kalra, and Gaubatz, needed help getting to this week’s game. They all say StubHub exercised its Fan Protect guarantee, which offers replacement seats-or-your money back at its “sole discretion.” They all say StubHub offered a refund. But the numbers don’t balance. In Molina’s case, a $2,200 refund is not enough to buy new row one seats in the upper deck, because prices have skyrocketed. “Literally like $10,000 each. There’s no way we’re gonna get back into the game,” she said. Same math problem for Gaubatz and Kalra. “The price of the ticket had gone 10x,” Kalra said. NBC Bay Area Responds checked. Yes, some ticket prices today are up to 10-times more than what people paid months ago. So, these viewers went looking for help. “I just typed the situation into the ChatGPT and it said, ‘OK, here’s the next thing you need to do: contact NBC,’” Gaubatz said. Molina and Kalra contacted NBC Bay Area Responds, too. “NBC actually helps consumers,” Kalra said. NBC Bay Area Responds then contacted StubHub. Next, it took action. StubHub gave Kalra’s family five replacement seats at no extra cost. “They are really super excited to go see the match now,” he said. Same success for Gaubatz:. Three replacement seats – together. “We are going,” he said. Finally, what about Molina’s so-called “upgrade” from row one to row 24? A StubHub rep specifically said, “We apologize for their poor experience.” StubHub moved Molina back to row one. She reached her goal, after asking us for an assist. “It worked, so I’m excited,” Molina said. Recently, California Attorney General Rob Bonta sent FIFA a letter demanding answers about its ticketing practices. So, NBC Bay Area Responds wondered if the AG was preparing to mail StubHub and other ticket re-sellers a similar letter. NBC Bay Area Responds asked, but the AG’s office did not respond.

headlines.title: Bay Area fans scramble for Team USA gear ahead of historic World Cup match
headlines.description: Team USA World Cup fever is sweeping the Bay Area as fans scramble for merchandise ahead of Wednesday’s Round of 32 match against Bosnia-Herzegovina. The highly anticipated knockout game at the San Francisco Bay Area Stadium in Santa Clara marks only the second time the region has hosted a U.S. World Cup match. Satyan Raju and his friend of more than 35 years, Manoj Panda, both of San Ramon, are among the lucky few who secured tickets to the historic match. “Absolutely a home game,” Panda said. “Soccer and football is very popular. We grew up playing, and it’s good to see. We’ve been watching on television for a long time, but this is the first time I’m watching in the stadium.” For Raju, Wednesday will be a World Cup homecoming. He was in the stands 32 years ago when the U.S. played in the Bay Area’s only other domestic World Cup game — a 1-0 loss to eventual champion Brazil in the 1994 Round of 16 at Stanford Stadium. “I went to the Brazil game, and it was in Stanford Stadium, and I’m looking forward to this game,” Raju said. While he admits he doesn’t remember many details from the 1994 match other than the excitement of the crowd, Raju said he and his three friends are ready to represent the U.S. this time around. After a desperate search across the Bay Area, the group finally found the Team USA jerseys they had been looking for at a pop-up store in Livermore. “I’m very excited,” Raju said. “We went to a lot of other shops, like Dick’s Sporting Goods, and everywhere is sold out.” Jennifer Shipe, the manager of the FIFA World Cup pop-up store at the Livermore Premium Outlets, said the location is constantly pulling inventory from the back as U.S. apparel flies off the shelves. “We’ve actually restocked throughout the whole entire day,” Shipe said. “We don’t want any leftover space in between.” Shipe noted that fans are buying in bulk, pointing out one family that purchased more than 15 jerseys and official uniforms just before Raju arrived. With merchandise selling out across the region, Wednesday’s crowd is expected to be a sea of red, white and blue. Raju and his friends plan to be right in the middle of it, raising the decibel level for the home team. “We’re looking forward to it,” Raju said. “Big party tomorrow at Levi’s.”

headlines.title: Head of San Francisco Chinese Chamber of Commerce resigns amid scandalous legal disputes
headlines.description: President of the San Francisco Chinese Chamber of Commerce Donald Luu has resigned from his influential position as ongoing legal battles embroil one of the oldest Chinese American business organizations in the United States. According to Luu’s attorney, his decision to step down partly stems from “unsubstantiated rumors and gossip” that were becoming a distraction for the chamber. I made the difficult decision to resign from my position as President of the Board of the San Francisco Chinese Chamber of Commerce so that I could focus on my family and not allow an employee’s false allegations against me to distract from the essential work of the chamber. Donald Luu, former president of the San Francisco Chinese Chamber of Commerce “I made the difficult decision to resign from my position as President of the Board of the San Francisco Chinese Chamber of Commerce so that I could focus on my family and not allow an employee’s false allegations against me to distract from the essential work of the chamber,” Luu said in a statement to NBC Bay Area’s Investigative Unit. The chamber, which boasts a storied history spanning more than a century, remains one of San Francisco’s most powerful and influential civic institutions, hosting some of the city’s best-known cultural events, including the Chinese New Year Parade. Donald Luu, president of the San Francisco Chinese Chamber of Commerce, resigned from his post on June 8, three weeks prior to when his term was set to expire. Donald Luu resigned three weeks before his term was set to expire Luu’s term as president was set to expire Tuesday, but the longtime activist and community organizer resigned more than three weeks early. Luu said he remains an active member of the chamber’s board of directors. This all comes amid accusations from a chamber employee, Judy Lee. According to a statement from her attorney, Lee is alleging sexual harassment, unwelcome advances, and a hostile work environment during her time with the chamber. Ms. Lee was forced to endure years of unwelcome advances and retaliatory behavior, as well as the Chinese Chamber’s refusal to properly investigate or remediate the misconduct, as required both by law and by common decency. Kathleen Alparce, attorney for chamber employee Judy Lee “Ms. Lee voiced her complaints numerous times directly to the Chinese Chamber before taking this step, yet the organization’s leadership took no action to stop the abuse,” her attorney Kathleen Alparce wrote in a statement. “Ms. Lee was forced to endure years of unwelcome advances and retaliatory behavior, as well as the Chinese Chamber’s refusal to properly investigate or remediate the misconduct, as required both by law and by common decency.” Lee, who remains an employee of the chamber, also sits on San Francisco’s Building Inspection Commission, which is responsible for overseeing construction permits and building safety throughout the city. Judy Lee, an employee of the San Francisco Chinese Chamber of Commerce, is alleging she endured sexual harassment, retaliation, and a hostile work environment. Alparce said for legal reasons her client isn’t saying who exactly at the chamber she is accusing of impropriety. Luu denies any involvement. “Additionally, to protect myself against these inaccurate claims, I have filed legal action against the employee,” Luu said in a statement. Luu’s attorney provided NBC Bay Area with a copy of his suit and a court receipt showing it was officially submitted to the court. The suit accuses Lee of extortion and defamation, saying she “fabricated felony accusations” against him. Luu also accuses Lee of violating a “confidential settlement agreement” they both entered into, alleging Lee tried to weaponize “its very existence.” The lawsuit does not detail what exactly was included in the settlement agreement. AAPI community May 31, 2023 Investigation Feb 19, 2025 Allegations of a hidden camera NBC Bay Area has also learned of a separate lawsuit, filed by three other chamber employees against the chamber itself, which accuses Judy Lee of setting up a hidden camera inside the office. The lawsuit, filed by Lainar Chan, Mavis Ngo, and Christine Hui, accuses the Chinese Chamber of Commerce of “failing to properly monitor, supervise, or control its employee, Judy Lee, allowing her to install an unauthorized recording device with potential audio and video capture capabilities in the workplace.” “Plaintiffs suffered severe emotional distress, anxiety, loss of dignity, and a compromised sense of safety,” the lawsuit states. The complaint does not provide a reason as to why Lee would allegedly install a hidden camera inside the chamber office. Lee, through her attorney, denied the accusations and said the chamber failed to take appropriate action after Lee made numerous complaints about a hostile work environment. Ms. Lee had to take her personal safety and well-being into her own hands. Kathleen Alparce, attorney for chamber employee Judy Lee “In fact, instead of recognizing or acknowledging Ms. Lee as a victim, the Chinese Chamber and its leadership chose to villainize Ms. Lee,” said Alparce. “Ms. Lee had to take her personal safety and well-being into her own hands.” NBC Bay Area asked Alparce what was meant by saying Lee took matters “into her own hands,” and asked if that statement had anything to do with the allegation that Lee set up a hidden camera inside the chamber. “Based on ongoing legal matters and other legal considerations, we are unable to comment of provide additional information at this time,” she said. If you or someone you know is experiencing domestic violence, you can get help by calling the National Domestic Violence Hotline at 1-800-799-SAFE (7233). In San Francisco, you can also contact San Francisco Women Against Rape and the Mayor’s Office of Victim’s Rights for more resources. Contact The Investigative Unit submit tips | 1-888-996-TIPS | e-mail Bigad Follow @BigadShaban on social media Bigad Shaban

headlines.title: Alameda County supervisors unanimously approve policy-driven reparations plan for Black residents
headlines.description: After nearly three years of research and community engagement, the Alameda County Board of Supervisors unanimously passed a sweeping set of reparations recommendations Tuesday aimed at confronting the county’s history of racial inequity. While reparations are often associated with direct cash payments, the Alameda County Reparations Commission’s plan takes a different approach. The 43 phased recommendations task the board with implementing policies designed to create lasting institutional change rather than issuing individual checks. The commission is now calling on county leaders to put the recommendations into action. Initial steps include creating a permanent committee and launching an equity assessment. The plan’s immediate priorities focus heavily on housing and economic justice, including closing the wealth gap through support for Black-owned businesses, offering down payment assistance, and addressing the ongoing impacts of redlining and displacement. It also calls for targeted investments in Black youth and mental health. Long-term goals include restitution for wrongful convictions, broader criminal justice reform and expanding access to education. “Our expectation is the opportunities that exist for you are the same for everybody else—the ability to buy a home, to get the job, to get the training, to get healthcare,” said Debra Gore, chair of the Alameda County Reparations Commission. Gore emphasized the importance of local government taking accountability. “It’s shifting the way that it looks at the harms it does and the repairs to make,” Gore said. “That shift is overdue. It has to be done at the county level.” Alameda County Supervisor Nate Miley, who spearheaded the commission with support from Supervisor Elisa Márquez, said the plan provides a critical foundation for future legislation. “These collective efforts produce research, recommendations and a framework for repair that will inform county policy and contribute to ongoing efforts to remedy systemic inequities and improve outcomes,” Miley said. The recommendations were shaped by data and community listening sessions, including forums hosted by the Black Cultural Zone. Supporters said the plan needed to be rooted in resident input and driven by those with lived experiences of racial inequity. Carolyn Johnson, CEO of the Black Cultural Zone, said officially adopting the recommendations is a crucial step toward accountability. “When you do this, no one can forget, you can’t wipe it away, you can’t hide it,” Johnson said. “It is on the record, and the efforts that we all believe are necessary are on the record, and I believe that’s the most important thing.” Although the adopted plan currently has no set price tag, advocates at Tuesday’s meeting stressed that true healing will require a tangible, well-funded commitment from the county, declaring that the time for study has concluded and the era of active repair has begun.

headlines.title: San Francisco community picks up pieces, seeks cause after devastating church fire
headlines.description: Members of a historic church in San Francisco’s Pacific Heights neighborhood are picking up the pieces following a devastating fire that shattered stained-glass windows, destroyed the roof and caused the partial collapse of an exterior wall. More than 100 firefighters responded to the blaze at the Seventh-day Adventist church, a neighborhood landmark since the 1890s. San Francisco Fire Chief Dean Crispen said the church’s size and age created special challenges for crews. “Some of them have never been to a fire of this magnitude before,” Crispen said. “So we did learn a lot about the capabilities of our auxiliary water supply system. We were able to pull water from a high-pressure water system.” Crispen noted the high-pressure nozzles sprayed brown water due to sediment in the system’s reservoirs and tanks. Fire units remained on the scene the following day to monitor for flare-ups and assess the structural integrity of the collapsed wall. Investigators are still working to determine the cause of the fire, including whether it was connected to a renovation project underway at the church. The fire also caused collateral damage to the building next door, displacing residents and temporarily shuttering Eliza’s Restaurant, a family business that opened about 35 years ago. The restaurant owners, who live in an apartment above the business, safely evacuated with an infant but sustained significant water damage to their home. Crispen toured the damage with Emily, a family member of the restaurant’s owners. “We’re definitely closed this entire week, but once we are open back up please, we encourage you to come,” Emily said. “Send them some love. This is a family business, it’s all my uncles and aunts, my parents.” The church’s lead pastor spent much of the day on site assessing the ruins. According to a prepared statement, the congregation will join a nearby Seventh-day Adventist church for its July 4 services.
